serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Hong Shao Tofu is approximately 150g, equivalent to one medium portion as served in a Singaporean zi char or home-cooked meal.

nutrient_source.title: Hong Shao Tofu — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hong Shao Tofu (CR ID: F09050006). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this primary source with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Soy-Based and Tofu-Based Foods — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Hong Shao Tofu in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated based on the closest analogous food: plain tofu and soy-based dishes, which consistently register GI values of 15 or below in peer-reviewed literature (Brand-Miller et al., University of Sydney GI Database). Carbohydrate content is extremely low at 0.3g/100g, making the glycemic load negligible. GL per 150g serving = (15 × 0.3 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.07, rounded to 0.2 for conservatism. This is a defensible low-GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 222mg/100g from soy sauce and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 222mg/100g is in the borderline range; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–120g per meal, request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation, and balance with high-potassium vegetables to mitigate cumulative sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.3g/100g), negligible sugar (0g/100g), high protein (8.8g/100g), and low GI (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hong Shao Tofu is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content, negligible glycaemic load, and high protein content that slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial blood glucose stability; ensure braising sauce is prepared without added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (11.2g/100g) with estimated low saturated fat (~1.8g/100g); sodium at 222mg/100g warrants monitoring
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile of Hong Shao Tofu is predominantly unsaturated (sesame oil, tofu lipids), with estimated saturated fat of ~1.8g/100g, which is acceptable for cardiovascular health; however, sodium at 222mg/100g should be managed through reduced-sodium soy sauce and portion control to limit cumulative cardiovascular sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients (ginger, garlic, sesame oil) and low saturated fat (~1.8g/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hong Shao Tofu contains ginger (gingerols) and garlic (allicin) with anti-inflammatory properties, and tofu provides calcium (76mg/100g) and iron (3mg/100g) beneficial for bone and joint health; use reduced-sodium soy sauce to limit sodium-related tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hong Shao Tofu (红烧豆腐, literally 'red-braised tofu') is a classic Chinese and Chinese-Singaporean dish in which deep-fried tofu is stir-fried and braised together with pork meat, seafood, beaten eggs, dried mushrooms, and vegetables in a savoury soy-based sauce. The dish is named for the characteristic reddish-brown colour imparted by soy sauce and sometimes oyster sauce or Shaoxing wine. The texture contrasts the crispy exterior of the deep-fried tofu with a soft, flavour-absorbing interior, surrounded by tender pork, springy seafood, and silky egg. It is commonly served as a main dish in Singaporean zi char (煮炒) restaurants and home kitchens, typically paired with steamed white rice. Nutritionally, Hong Shao Tofu is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing 8.8g protein, 11.2g fat, and only 0.3g carbohydrate per 100g, with 137 kcal/100g. It is an excellent source of calcium (76mg/100g) and iron (3mg/100g) from tofu. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 222mg/100g warrants portion awareness for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to pork and seafood content, and contains soy, egg, and seafood allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F09050006) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match to the canonical Singapore HPB entry for Hong Shao Tofu. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for tofu-based dishes, clearly documented.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Hong Shao Tofu preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. saturated_fat_g of 1.8g/100g is estimated based on typical tofu and pork fat composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (tofu, fried; pork, cooked) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 3. sugar_g is reported as 0.0g/100g by HPB — this is a confirmed zero per the approved source. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for plain tofu and soy-based foods; no direct GI measurement exists for Hong Shao Tofu. Glycaemic load per 150g serving calculated as (15 × 0.3 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.07, conservatively reported as 0.2. 5. The input record described the dish as containing 'deep-fried tofu' but also listed 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' dietary tags — these have been removed as the dish contains pork and seafood. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for Hong Shao Tofu as of 2024.
